findings.description: BFS in reachable() enqueues neighbors without consulting the seen set, causing an infinite loop (100% CPU, never returns) on the cyclic graphs the docstring claims to support and exponential O(#paths) re-enqueueing on DAGs with shared substructure instead of O(V+E); fix: guard with `if neighbor not in seen` before appending (also a correctness bug — detail deferred to critic-logic).

count_paths() recurses with no memoization, making runtime O(number of paths) — roughly 2^(n/2) calls on diamond-chain DAGs — where an lru_cache or single DP pass over the existing topological_order() yields O(V+E).

count_paths() recursion depth equals the longest dependency chain, so graphs deeper than Python's ~1000-frame limit crash with RecursionError; an iterative DP removes both this scalability ceiling and the memoization gap.

Read paths index the defaultdict directly (lines 30, 40, 54, 61), auto-vivifying a permanent empty list for every sink or unknown node queried — read-only operations mutate and bloat _edges by O(nodes) wasted entries on large graphs; use `self._edges.get(node, ())` in readers.
add_node() emits an INFO log per node, so bulk-loading millions of nodes becomes dominated by logging I/O when handlers are attached; log at DEBUG or batch-summarize instead.

add_edge() appends without dedup, so repeated registration of the same dependency grows adjacency lists without bound and multiplies the cost of every traversal (compounding the line 31 and line 41 blowups).
neighbors() returns a fresh O(degree) defensive copy on every call, which is wasteful only in tight loops — micro-optimization at best.
